title: How do I start learning to code with zero experience at 19?
body: I'm 19 and I keep hearing that coding is a great skill, but every time I try to start I have no idea what language to pick or where to even begin. I have a laptop and a lot of free time this summer. What's an actual realistic starting point, not just 'learn Python' with no direction?

title: I went from retail worker to junior developer in 14 months — here's exactly what I did
body: Three years ago I was folding shirts at a clothing store with zero plan for my future. I want to share the exact path I took to become a junior developer, including the free resources I used, the mistakes that cost me months, and the project that actually got me hired. This isn't a 'just believe in yourself' post — it's the boring, specific roadmap.

title: Is it normal to feel exhausted all the time even after 8 hours of sleep?
body: I sleep a full 8 hours most nights but I still wake up feeling like I haven't slept at all. This has been going on for a few months. I don't think it's just 'being a teenager' anymore. Should I actually be worried, or is this just normal at my age?

title: What I wish every patient under 25 understood about heart health
body: I see so many young patients who think heart health is 'an old person problem.' It isn't. The habits you build right now are the strongest predictor of how your heart performs in your 40s and 50s. Here are the four things I tell every young patient in my clinic, and none of them involve giving up the foods you love.

title: I got rejected from 40 internships. What am I doing wrong?
body: I've applied to over 40 internships this year and gotten maybe 3 interviews and zero offers. My GPA is fine, my resume looks okay to me, but clearly something isn't working. I genuinely don't know if it's my resume, my interviews, or just bad luck at this point.

title: The one interview question that decides if you get hired (and how to answer it)
body: After reading thousands of resumes and sitting in hundreds of interviews, I noticed one question quietly decides more outcomes than any other: 'Tell me about a time you failed.' Most candidates either dodge it or oversell a fake failure. Here's the structure that actually works, with a real example answer.

title: How do you set boundaries with a friend who only talks about themselves?
body: I have a friend I genuinely care about, but every conversation somehow becomes about her problems. I've tried gently bringing up my own stuff and she just steers it back. I don't want to end the friendship but I'm exhausted. How do people actually set boundaries without it turning into a huge confrontation?

title: I almost lost my best friend over jealousy — here's what saved the friendship
body: When my best friend started succeeding at things I was struggling with, I let jealousy quietly poison our friendship for almost a year. I want to share the uncomfortable conversation that fixed it, because I almost didn't have it, and almost lost ten years of friendship over my own ego.

title: I'm 22 and have $0 saved. Where do I even start?
body: I just got my first real paycheck job and I genuinely have no savings, no idea what a budget should look like, and a vague fear of credit cards. Every finance article assumes you already know the basics. Where does someone with literally zero financial knowledge actually start?

title: The simplest budgeting method that actually works for people who hate budgeting
body: Most budgeting advice assumes you'll enjoy tracking every coffee you buy. You won't, and that's fine. I use a single rule with my younger clients that takes five minutes a month and actually sticks. No spreadsheets, no apps, no guilt spirals.

title: How do I deal with constant comparison anxiety from social media?
body: I know logically that social media isn't real life, but I still spiral every time I open Instagram and see people my age who seem to have everything figured out. It's affecting how I feel about my own life even when things are objectively fine. How do you actually break this cycle, not just 'delete the app' advice?

title: Burnout isn't laziness — what therapists actually want you to know
body: So many young clients come to me convinced they're just lazy or weak, when what they're actually describing is textbook burnout. The two are treated completely differently, and confusing them makes recovery much harder. Here's how to tell the difference and what actually helps.

title: How do I find my own art style instead of copying others?
body: Every time I draw something I love, I realize it looks exactly like another artist I follow. I want to develop something that feels like mine, but I don't even know what that would look like. How do people actually find their own style instead of just remixing their influences forever?

title: I posted my art publicly every day for a year. Here's what changed
body: A year ago my art looked nothing like it does now, and the biggest jump didn't come from a course or a tutorial — it came from the discomfort of posting daily and watching my own patterns. Here's what actually happened to my style, my confidence, and my speed over 365 days.

title: How do I stop procrastinating on tasks I actually care about?
body: This is the confusing part — I don't procrastinate on things I don't care about, I procrastinate hardest on the things that actually matter to me. It's like the more something means to me, the longer I avoid starting it. Does anyone else experience this, and how do you break it?

title: I moved out at 18 with no help from family — these are the life skills nobody taught me
body: Nobody sat me down and explained how renters insurance works, how to read a lease, or what to do the first time a landlord ignores your messages. I learned all of it the hard way over two years. Here's the practical list I wish someone had handed me on day one.
